back_translation: Josiah kept the Passover to the LORD in Jerusalem, and they slaughtered the Passover lamb on the fourteenth day of the first month.
He appointed the priests to their duties and encouraged them in the service of the house of the LORD.

He said to the Levites who taught all Israel and were holy to the LORD, “Put the holy ark in the house that Solomon son of David king of Israel built. You need not carry it on your shoulders. Now serve the LORD your God and His people Israel.
Prepare yourselves by your fathers' houses according to your divisions, as prescribed in the writing of David king of Israel and in the document of Solomon his son.
Stand in the Holy Place according to the groupings of the fathers' houses of your kinsmen the lay people, and according to the division of the fathers' houses of the Levites.
Slaughter the Passover lamb, consecrate yourselves, and make preparations for your brothers, to act according to the word of the LORD given through Moses.”
Josiah contributed to the lay people who were present flocks of lambs and young goats, 30,000 in number, and 3,000 bulls; all these were from the king's own possessions, for the Passover offerings.

His officials contributed willingly to the people, the priests, and the Levites. Hilkiah, Zechariah, and Jehiel, the chief officials of the house of God, gave the priests 2,600 Passover offerings and 300 bulls.
Conaniah, with his brothers Shemaiah and Nethanel, and Hashabiah, Jeiel, and Jozabad, the chiefs of the Levites, gave the Levites 5,000 Passover offerings and 500 bulls.
When the service had been prepared, the priests stood in their places and the Levites in their divisions, according to the king's command.
They slaughtered the Passover lambs, and the priests sprinkled the blood they received, while the Levites skinned the animals.

They set aside the burnt offerings to give them to the groupings of the fathers' houses of the lay people, to offer to the LORD as it is written in the Book of Moses; and they did the same with the bulls.
They roasted the Passover lamb with fire according to the rule, and they boiled the holy offerings in pots, kettles, and bowls, and quickly brought them to all the people.

Afterward they made preparations for themselves and for the priests, because the priests, the sons of Aaron, were busy offering the burnt offerings and the fat until night; so the Levites made preparations for themselves and for the priests, the sons of Aaron.

The singers, the sons of Asaph, were at their stations according to the command of David, Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun the king's seer; and the gatekeepers were at each gate. They did not need to leave their service, because their brothers the Levites prepared for them.
So all the service of the LORD was prepared that day, to keep the Passover and to offer burnt offerings on the altar of the LORD, according to the command of King Josiah.
The children of Israel who were present kept the Passover at that time, and the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days.

No Passover like it had been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet. None of the kings of Israel had kept a Passover like the one Josiah kept, with the priests, the Levites, all Judah and Israel who were present, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
This Passover was kept in the eighteenth year of the reign of Josiah.
After all this, when Josiah had set the temple in order, Neco king of Egypt went up to fight at Carchemish by the Euphrates, and Josiah went out against him.

But Neco sent messengers to him, saying, “What is between you and me, O king of Judah? I have not come against you today, but against the dynasty with which I am at war. God has commanded me to hurry. Stop opposing God, who is with me, lest He destroy you.”
But Josiah did not turn away from him; instead he disguised himself to fight against him. He did not listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God, but went to fight on the Plain of Megiddo.
The archers shot King Josiah, and the king said to his servants, “Take me away, for I am badly wounded.”

So his servants took him out of that chariot, placed him in his second chariot, and brought him to Jerusalem. He died and was buried in the tombs of his fathers, and all Judah and Jerusalem mourned for Josiah.

Jeremiah composed a lament for Josiah, and all the male and female singers have spoken of Josiah in their laments to this day. They made these a custom in Israel, and they are written in the Book of Laments.
As for the rest of the acts of Josiah and his deeds of loving devotion, according to what is written in the Law of the LORD,
his acts from beginning to end—they are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah.
